# Questions in Livestream 190

Live Stream #190: Spilt Tea All Over the Floor

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=489" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:09</a> | Did the worn temple steps at Hathor actually melt? | He said no. They are eroded stone, worn down by sand and foot traffic rather than heat or anything exotic. | He corrected the premise first: the steps are not granite, but sandstone or limestone-like stone. Their melted-looking shape comes from ordinary erosion, because blown sand is ground into the softer stone by people using the stairs. He granted that the effect looks strange, but said it is not melting and does not require aliens or any exotic explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=570" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:30</a> | Is it wrong for Christians to amass wealth and enjoy luxury if some of that wealth is also used for God's kingdom? | He said wealth itself is not the problem; the issue is selfish, hedonistic, or unethical use of it rather than stewarding it under God. | He framed wealth, talents, and opportunities as stewardship from God. Enjoying a blessing is not automatically sinful, but wealth becomes a problem when it is pursued or used only for pleasure, turns into hedonism, or is obtained unethically. He invoked Calvin's idea of working, saving, and giving as much as possible, and said buying something nice can be permissible when generosity and ministry support are still present. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:24</a> | How can the late date of the Exodus be reconciled with Acts 13:20? | He said Acts 13:20 is a difficult chronology text in general, not a problem that the early-date Exodus solves. | He explained that commentators disagree about where the 450 years begins and ends. Some calculations try to combine the time in Egypt, the wilderness period, and conquest, while another approach connects it to the Abrahamic promise and the 430 years of Exodus 12, with additional time in Haran. His main point was that the math is debated and unclear either way, so Acts 13:20 does not uniquely undermine a late-date Exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=1098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:18</a> | Did `I AM` mean anything special to the Hebrews before God sent Moses? | He said no special divine meaning is evident before Moses, and treated it more as a verbal form than as an earlier sacred name among the Hebrews. | He said it appears to have been a verbal tense or form rather than a phrase with established divine significance before Moses. He noted that the Israelites' likely Amorite-related language background is hard to investigate because so few Amorite texts survive, so the grammatical details remain uncertain. On the available evidence, he did not see a special divine meaning prior to the Moses episode. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=1164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:24</a> | Is Molech a deity or a type of child sacrifice, is Genesis 22 an example of a Molech sacrifice motif, and was child sacrifice common in Ur? | He treated Molech as a type of sacrifice rather than a deity, did not directly address Genesis 22, and said Ur has evidence of human sacrifice but not the same tophet pattern. | He said Molech is best understood as a kind of child sacrifice, essentially a burnt offering, rather than a god; he emphasized that there are no inscriptions or names that clearly show Molech was worshiped as a deity. He did not give a clear direct answer on whether Genesis 22 is a Molech motif. For Ur, he said child sacrifice had been outlawed and there is no known tophet evidence like Carthage or possibly Canaan, though Ur does have evidence for human sacrifice around royal tombs, especially servants in the Ur III context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=1581" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:21</a> | How should someone balance apologetics, responding to the Hebrew Roots movement, and ordinary life when it feels overwhelming? | He advised not spreading oneself too thin, but discerning what God is calling the person to engage. | He agreed that Christ is the answer, but said one person cannot save everyone or take responsibility for the whole world. His practical advice was to seek balance, choose the work God is calling and giving passion for, and let God's grace take care of the rest. He framed apologetics or engagement with the Hebrew Roots movement as possible callings, not obligations to carry every burden at once. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=1865" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:05</a> | What books or resources are good for Sumerian or Akkadian study? | He recommended good language texts, especially Ungnad for Akkadian, and noted that the Chicago Assyrian Dictionary is available online. | He advised getting solid language texts if pursuing Assyriology. For Akkadian he specifically recommended Ungnad, while saying he was less sure what to recommend for Sumerian because he had not taken Sumerian. He also pointed to the Chicago Assyrian Dictionary as a standard work, noting that much of it can be downloaded for free as PDFs through the University of Chicago's ancient Near Eastern resources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=1974" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:54</a> | Why would Job be Syrian if he speaks of Yahweh? | He said Yahweh worship spread beyond Israelites in the Iron Age, so a Syrian or Hurrian setting for Job could still involve Yahweh. | He argued that Yahweh worship did get around in the Iron Age, citing biblical examples of non-Israelites encountering Yahweh worship. He suggested that migrating groups, including Hurrians after the breakup of Mitanni, could have contacted Israelites or Yahweh worshipers and carried that worship back toward Syria. On that model, Job's association with Yahweh does not rule out a Syrian background. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=2097" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:57</a> | Why can Akkadian not be written easily on parchment? | He explained that cuneiform is a stamped writing system made for clay and a stylus, not a flowing ink or parchment medium. | He demonstrated that Akkadian cuneiform is produced by pressing a stylus into clay with varying pressure, so the writing system fits a soft clay medium. That makes parchment a poor fit, because the signs are not simply drawn lines. He qualified that some later Neo-Assyrian-style signs could be approximated more easily with pencil and paper, but old Babylonian-style cuneiform belongs much more naturally on clay. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=2472" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:12</a> | In Exodus 16:3, did Israelite slaves actually eat meat and bread in Egypt? | He said they did have meat and bread, but as state-owned slaves they were expected to cultivate or produce much of it themselves. | He read the complaint in Exodus as referring to real food access, not comfort or abundance. In his explanation, state-owned slaves could have meat and bread, but much of it would come through their own labor: cultivating grain, tending small animals, and being provided for as a work force. The point was not that Egypt was pleasant, but that the wilderness hunger made remembered Egyptian food seem attractive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=2682" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:42</a> | Why not stream Indiana Jones the way he streamed Assassin's Creed? | He said it is not likely unless someone sponsors it at consulting-rate levels, because it is not the best use of his time. | He said the prior Assassin's Creed stream happened because it was sponsored and that he would not have chosen it voluntarily. He joked about becoming some people's top gaming streamer, but then answered directly that gaming streams would have to replace other work and are not likely unless funded at his consulting rates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=2861" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:41</a> | What is John Walton's view of demons? | He said Walton treats demons as literary inventions rather than real beings, and he strongly disagrees. | He summarized Walton's view as saying demons are not real but function as literary devices. He called that dangerous, while also warning against the opposite extreme of obsessing over demons or blaming them for everything. His position was that spiritual realities exist, but demons are not omnipotent, all-knowing, or responsible for ordinary human sins. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=3171" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:51</a> | Does he believe in exorcisms, or are they all fake role-playing? | He said exorcisms are real and can be effective, though possession must be distinguished carefully from mental health issues. | He said he has seen enough supernatural material to believe demons and possession are real, so exorcism is possible. At the same time, he emphasized that responsible exorcists proceed cautiously because apparent possession can be confused with medical or psychiatric conditions. He also pointed to New Testament examples and historical practice as reasons not to dismiss exorcism as entirely fake. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=3317" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:17</a> | Does watching many Egyptology videos, or the fact that the title is not legally protected, make someone an Egyptologist? | He said no; within Egyptology, a PhD is the field's threshold for being recognized as an Egyptologist. | He explained that each discipline sets its own internal standards for professional competence. In Egyptology, unlike some areas of archaeology, the terminal degree is treated as the threshold. Watching videos, holding only a master's degree, or relying on the title being legally unprotected does not make someone an Egyptologist according to the field's own standards. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=3597" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:57</a> | Does Egyptology have sub-specializations? | Yes. He listed field archaeologists, linguists, historians, ceramists, and other narrower tracks within Egyptology. | He said Egyptology has many internal tracks, not one generic role. He listed field Egyptologists who primarily dig, linguists who work on texts and language, historians such as Kenneth Kitchen, and ceramists whose Egyptological work may involve lab analysis of pottery. He also stressed that these specializations exist inside a field with its own professional threshold for who counts as an Egyptologist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=3965" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:05</a> | Was the golden calf completely gold, or wood with a gold layer on top? | He said it was probably completely gold, or at least completely metal, and much smaller than popular depictions. | He argued that the golden calf was the central idol understood as the deity's true substance, so it was probably not wood plated with gold. He pictured it as small, perhaps only six to eight inches, made from leftover donated gold after tabernacle contributions. Later in the discussion he added that it was likely hammered or cold-formed more than cast as a large statue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=4324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:04</a> | What antivirus program does the Faulk family use? | He said Windows Defender is usually enough, with Malwarebytes sometimes useful for cleanup, and criticized many antivirus suites as worse than helpful. | He answered that they use Windows Defender and regard it as sufficient for normal use. If a system has to be cleaned after a problem, he said Malwarebytes can be useful. He was sharply critical of many commercial antivirus programs, treating them as bloated or scam-like enough that they can become part of the problem rather than a helpful protection layer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=4412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:32</a> | Is "carpenter" the best translation for Jesus' occupation, or was he more likely a stonemason? | He said "carpenter" is a fine translation because `tekton` primarily points to woodwork, though it can be broader. | He explained that the Greek word can mean carpenter, woodworker, or more generally builder, with some extended uses for masons, sculptors, or smiths. He said the claim that Jesus was a stonemason is an inference from location near construction activity, not the basic meaning of the term. He preferred keeping the focus on skilled woodwork while acknowledging that ancient carpenters also made tools and did broader building work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=4656" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:36</a> | Was Jonah's prayer said while he was dying or after he died? | He said it was while he was alive, pointing to Jonah 2's wording and past-tense literary features. | He walked through Jonah 2 and said the prayer is spoken from the fish while Jonah is in the throes of death. The past-tense language describes his descent toward Sheol, with the turning point around the line about being brought up from the pit. He allowed that whether the prayer was finished in Sheol is hard to say, but most of it is framed as Jonah praying from the belly of the fish on the way toward death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=4930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:10</a> | Could 1 Peter 3:18-20 suggest the spirits in prison had a chance to trust Christ? | He treated the passage cautiously and said the text can suggest proclamation, but it does not clearly establish a simple second-chance doctrine. | He connected 1 Peter 3 and 1 Peter 4 to what he called second-chance theory: the idea that some who never had a real opportunity to pledge allegiance to Christ in life might receive such an opportunity after death. He said there is warrant for that view, but it is not universalism, because some could still reject Christ. He also emphasized that interpreters differ and that the Bible is not completely explicit about the fate of people who never had that opportunity while alive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=5211" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:51</a> | Could a postmortem opportunity extend to those who did not know God? | He said it could, but not without limits. | He said the idea could extend to people who did not know God, including examples later raised such as children who die young. But he also limited it: someone who had knowledge of Yahweh and chose another god in disobedience would not fit the same category. He tied the answer to confidence in God's justice and grace rather than to a fully explicit biblical mechanism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=5362" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:22</a> | If Genesis 1:1 is a title separate from verse 2, why does Genesis 1:2 begin with a waw? | He said the Hebrew can still work as a simple waw and does not require verse 1 to be read as a dependent clause. | He checked the Hebrew and said the waw in Genesis 1:2 is a simple conjunction, not a consecutive waw that would force a dependent clause. Therefore Genesis 1:1 can still function as an independent title-like clause, followed by another independent clause joined by "and," "now," or even "but" depending on translation. What he rejected was translating verse 2 as though it were subordinate to verse 1 with wording like "since" or "after." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=5950" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:10</a> | Could there be other eternal fundamental concepts God used to make the universe, such as numbers, logic, shapes, or truth? | He said there could be, but identifying such concepts from our perspective is extremely difficult. | He distinguished between whether God could use such concepts and whether he would have done so, saying he saw no reason it was impossible. He then stressed the difficulty of grasping foundational realities because creation itself can obscure the concepts behind it. He used examples such as the problem of defining nothingness and the non-emptiness of space to show how hard these questions become. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=6308" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:08</a> | Was the average person in the pew around 1700 more biblically knowledgeable than today's pew? | He said yes, because lay theological reading was far more common in that period. | He said the 1600s and 1700s had a large amount of biblical and theological literature written for ordinary readers, especially short tracts. He argued that many such works disappeared because they were read until they wore out and were discarded, while unread elite books were more likely to survive. For him, that survival pattern helps explain why earlier lay biblical literacy can be underestimated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=7089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:09</a> | Are Canadian mail problems usually theft or incompetence? | He said they are generally incompetence rather than theft. | He contrasted the Canadian postal system with places where theft may be more common, saying Canadian mail problems are usually caused by incompetence. The exchange came after examples of late or missing packages and checks, and he treated the system as unreliable even when it is functioning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=7196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:56</a> | Why do some people think intelligent aliens would disprove all religions? | He said they assume that because the Bible does not discuss aliens, it must deny their existence, but that is a narrow reading. | He said the Bible makes no claim one way or the other about extraterrestrial life because that is outside the scope of the text. In his view, intelligent aliens would not disprove Christianity: they might have their own revelation, might not have fallen, might not know God yet, or might fit another category. He also rejected the idea that aliens must be demons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=7417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:37</a> | How do UPS and FedEx compare in Canada? | He said FedEx is good but expensive, while UPS is terrible in Canada. | He contrasted Canadian courier experiences, saying FedEx works well but costs a lot, while UPS and DHL are very bad in Canada. He gave an example of UPS failing to deliver to a UPS Store mailbox and described brokerage fees on a small shirt order as excessive enough that he refused delivery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=7846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:46</a> | Were the Ten Commandments written prior to Moses or the Exodus? | He said there is no evidence that the Ten Commandments were written before Moses. | The original wording was unclear, but once it was clarified as asking about a pre-Mosaic or pre-Exodus origin, he answered that there is no evidence for that. He said the commandments reflect concerns from the Exodus period and that their formulation does not appear to be pre-Mosaic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=7962" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:42</a> | How should the story of David and Bathsheba be viewed; was it abuse? | He said David clearly abused power in taking another man's wife and arranging Uriah's death, though the text does not explicitly describe Bathsheba as coerced. | He said the story is certainly an abuse of power, especially in taking another man's wife and having Uriah killed. At the same time, he cautioned that the text does not explicitly show Bathsheba being coerced or exploited, so claiming that specific form of abuse goes beyond what the passage states. His conclusion was that David is unquestionably abusing his power, but the transcript-supported answer preserves uncertainty about Bathsheba's own agency because the text does not settle it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=8121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:21</a> | Why could David not control himself in the first place? | He said David could have, but he was a sinner with time on his hands and was looking where he should not have looked. | He rejected the premise that David was unable to control himself. David could have controlled himself, but he was sinful, idle, and looking where he should not have looked. The answer tied the failure to ordinary moral culpability rather than inevitability or lack of capacity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=8151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:51</a> | How common was it for a king to walk on his roof and see a woman bathing at night? | He said roof bathing could be common in some ancient settings, and Bathsheba was on her own roof taking a private bath. | He said bathing on a roof or in a semi-public setting could be normal in some cultures, but Bathsheba was on her own roof and had some expectation of privacy. David's palace roof was higher, giving him a view down into private areas below. He added that David was older, no longer useful in battle, idle at home, and should have looked away rather than dwelling on what he saw. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=8340" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:00</a> | If Amorites and Canaanites used terms like El and Elohim, did they share the same myths about Baal, Mot, Ashtart, and others? | He said it is possible but uncertain; shared divine names do not prove all the same myth cycles were held everywhere. | He said Amorites moved widely and used names containing El, but that does not prove they shared the full Canaanite or Ugaritic mythic system involving Baal, Mot, Ashtart, Yam, and others. Terms like El and related Semitic forms are much older and more widespread than the Ugaritic Baal cycle. The safest answer was that shared vocabulary may indicate overlap, but the exact mythic beliefs remain uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=8500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:40</a> | Did the patriarchs have a moral code from God, or did they follow the local laws where they lived? | He said Abraham and Sarah seem to quote or follow Mesopotamian legal ideas, but the answer may differ among patriarchs. | He said the answer depends on which patriarch is in view. Abraham and Sarah appear to be operating with Mesopotamian law codes, especially in the Hagar dispute, where their arguments sound like they are quoting or applying those laws. Isaac and Jacob, however, were born in Canaan and do not show the same legal-code discourse, so their ethical framework may have been more shaped by the local culture around them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=8673" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:33</a> | If Abraham quotes Mesopotamian law codes, is that evidence that his father was a man of power in Ur? | He said yes, it would be indirect supporting evidence. | He answered briefly that Abraham's apparent familiarity with Mesopotamian law codes could indirectly support the idea that his family had status or access to such legal culture. The answer stayed limited: it was supporting evidence, not a full independent argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=8716" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:16</a> | What resources are good for Egyptian culture and religious life before and during the Exodus? | He recommended his Ark of the Covenant book, Kenneth Kitchen, Old Testament background resources, and James Hoffmeier. | He named his own `The Ark of the Covenant in its Egyptian Context` as a useful resource, then added Kenneth Kitchen's `On the Reliability of the Old Testament`, an Old Testament backgrounds book, and James Hoffmeier's books on the Exodus, ancient Egypt, and the Levant. He also noted that Hoffmeier was working on a revised edition of `Israel in Egypt`, but did not know its status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=8900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:20</a> | What is your take on Elijah's departure in 2 Kings 2:11? | He said the chariot imagery is less central than Elijah being taken up to heaven, and the passage should be read carefully rather than over-systematized. | He emphasized that Elijah is taken to heaven and did not die in the ordinary sense. In his reading, the central point is not that Elijah rides the chariot, but that the whirlwind and heavenly imagery mark his being taken up. He later said the account should be read literally here, while avoiding speculative over-systematizing beyond what the text gives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=9603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:03</a> | What if the Hittites mentioned in the Pentateuch were Canaanites with the same name and had nothing to do with Anatolia? | He rejected that same-name theory and said the biblical Hittites should be connected with the Anatolian Hittites. | He said the idea does not make historical sense because the Hittites are known to have come into Canaan and Egypt fought them there. He compared the argument to other attempts to separate terms such as Amurru from Amorites, which he also rejects. For him, the extra-biblical evidence supports identifying the Hittites as Anatolians rather than unrelated Canaanites with the same name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=9785" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:05</a> | What are the origins of the Book of the Dead? | He said it comes from a long scribal trajectory of Egyptian funerary spells. | He described the Book of the Dead as a New Kingdom compilation of funerary spells written on papyrus. It developed from coffin texts of the Middle Kingdom, which in turn drew on pyramid texts from the Fifth and Sixth Dynasties. He stressed that this trajectory spans roughly 1,500 years, with related funerary texts also continuing on coffins. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=9916" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:16</a> | In Exodus 32:21-24, is Aaron's claim that the calf came out of the fire only a lame excuse, or is something about idol manufacture involved? | He said it is mainly a lie and a bad excuse, though it also fits a cultural pattern of blame-shifting excuses. | He read the passage as Aaron blaming the people and denying responsibility: he says he put gold in the fire and the calf simply appeared. He called that deception rather than a deep manufacturing detail. He compared the excuse to weak excuses in Egyptian tomb-robbery material and said Aaron may have expected a beating for the excuse, not the deaths that followed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=10429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:49</a> | How much time passed between Moses leaving Aaron in charge and returning from the mountain? | He said Moses was gone about a month, the forty days of the text, long enough for the people to think Moses and Yahweh were dead. | He connected the delay to the golden calf episode. Because Moses was away for roughly forty days, he said the people may have concluded that Moses had died and, in Egyptian categories where gods can die, that Yahweh had died too. In that setting, the golden calf functioned as a rebirth image for Yahweh rather than merely a random idol. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=10833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:33</a> | Is the boat-shaped formation on the mountain really Noah's Ark? | He said it is not; in his view it is one of several mud-flow formations and not evidence for an ark site. | He identified the formation as pareidolia and a mud flow rather than a boat. He said there is no evidence for petrified wood at the site, and that satellite or map views show multiple similar boat-shaped formations nearby. For him, the existence of many comparable formations undercuts the claim that this one is Noah's Ark. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=11009" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:29</a> | How can Leviticus 18:22 be reconciled with people, including Christians, who appear to be in loving relationships it condemns? | He declined to answer on the livestream because he said YouTube would take a dim view of that topic. | He did not give a substantive theological answer. He said this was one of the few areas he could not discuss on the channel because of platform limits, and suggested there might be other resources that could help. The transcript-supported answer is therefore a deferral rather than an argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=11073" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:33</a> | What would be the theological implications of alien life? | He said it would imply God created more life for relationship, much as God created humans and angels for relationship. | He said the main theological implication would be that God is a vast creator who made another kind of life for relationship. He compared alien life to humans and angels as created beings God could relate to, and said the scale of the universe makes such a possibility unsurprising to him. He also speculated only lightly that heaven and hell would be stranger places if alien life existed, but the core answer stayed with creation and relationship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=11390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:50</a> | Does the mention of Philistines in Genesis 21:34 help date the Exodus or the patriarchs, and how should their inclusion be interpreted? | He said it is a retrojection using later audience terminology, not evidence that Abraham lived among later Philistines. | He explained that Abraham predates the Philistine arrival in Canaan, so "land of the Philistines" is a later term used to orient the audience. He compared it to calling Joseph's Egyptian king "Pharaoh" or calling pre-Columbian lands "North America." In his view, the wording reflects the author's or audience's period, likely Moses or later, while identifying the coastal plain in terms later Israelites would understand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=11738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:38</a> | What should someone do if they keep getting the scholarship on an issue wrong? | He did not give a direct answer to that specific question. | The transcript shows the question being read, but the response moves immediately into thanks for the viewer's support and jokes about the year's topics. There is no clear advice in this segment about correcting one's scholarship beyond the surrounding livestream's broader emphasis on reading and learning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=11858" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:38</a> | Why are there no Egyptian records of the Exodus? | He said records from the relevant Delta sites rarely survive because of high water tables and poor preservation, especially for papyrus. | He gave two main reasons. First, the relevant Delta sites preserve poorly because the water table is high, so papyrus and other records decay rather than surviving. Second, Egyptian monumental records typically present successful deeds as offerings to the gods, so defeats, disasters, and embarrassments are often absent even when outside sources show they happened. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=12198" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:18</a> | Could Egyptians join Israel after seeing the plagues? | He said yes, pointing to the mixed multitude in Exodus 12 as including people who were not descendants of Jacob. | He pointed to Exodus 12:37-38 and the "mixed multitude" leaving Egypt with Israel. He defined that group as people who were not sons of Jacob, including Egyptians and other peoples living around Avaris or Goshen, such as Canaanites, Amorites, Libyans, Nubians, Hittites, and Hurrians. He said they could participate in Passover, join Israel, and eventually be integrated into the tribes, largely through marriage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=12377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:17</a> | What caused current biblical illiteracy? | He said literacy is cyclical and shaped by consumed media, and the current cycle has produced weak biblical literacy. | He said literacy rises and falls rather than increasing automatically, and the present period is on a downward cycle. He connected the decline to the media people consume: television, passive media, the internet, and short-form social media all reduce attention spans and reading habits. He contrasted that with earlier readers, including the church fathers, who expected long arguments and substantial memory from their audiences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=13479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:39</a> | Are the Exodus population numbers idealized or mistranslated? | He said the census numbers are controversial, and one major issue is how to interpret terms such as `eleph` in Numbers. | He said the census numbers in Numbers 2 and 26 are highly controversial, with at least nine interpretations. Options include taking the numbers literally, reading them as a Davidic census reconstruction, interpreting `eleph` as clans or social units, or seeing exaggeration shaped by war genre. He said the only broad consensus is that the simple literal interpretation is no longer viable, and he personally prefers a clan interpretation while acknowledging other views. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=13752" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:12</a> | How would you respond to the claim that only the Romans wanted to kill Jesus and the Pharisees were blameless? | He said the Romans were not the only ones involved; the Jewish authorities treated Jesus' claims as blasphemy and brought him first to the Sanhedrin. | He said Jesus was first brought to the Sanhedrin, where the Jewish authorities judged his claim to be God as blasphemy. Because Rome did not care about blasphemy, the charge had to be politicized as a claim to kingship against Caesar. In his account, both Pharisees and Sadducees were involved in the Sanhedrin process, the Romans executed Jesus as a political agitator, and neither side should be treated as wholly blameless. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=14343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:03</a> | Have there been "Gardiner was wrong about X" level discoveries in Egyptian linguistics? | He said yes: Gardiner was wrong to treat `sedjem.n=f` as always simple past tense. | He said later work showed that `sedjem.n=f` can function not only as simple past tense, but also in a second-tense construction, such as a "just as" clause leading into another clause. He stressed that Gardiner got a great deal right and still produced a valuable reference grammar, but this tense issue is a major point where modern Egyptian linguistics differs from him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=14665" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:25</a> | Does he miss the furniture store? | He said no, because current work is better for his back and allows more consistent writing and content. | He answered directly that he does not miss the furniture store. He pointed instead to current projects: a peer-reviewed article, two novels, and more consistent channel content. He also said leaving that work has been better on his back, though the exchange included a brief aside about strange experiences at the store. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=14777" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:06:17</a> | Will the novel be available in ebook or audiobook format? | He said it would definitely be available as an ebook, paperback, and hardcover, while audiobook format was still uncertain. | He said the novel was with beta readers, with pre-sale expected in January and publication in April. Ebook availability was definite, along with softcover and hardcover editions. Audiobook remained only a possibility because they would need to decide how to record it, possibly by reading it themselves, and that would take time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QhyTbeOS4g4?t=14863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:43</a> | Do shadowmen appear in the novels? | He said no, not yet, though other spirit creatures do. | He answered that shadowmen do not appear in the novels, at least not yet. He added that other spirit creatures do appear, but he did not want to give those details away. |
